findings.description: Path traversal / arbitrary file write: session.filename (from start(), unvalidated beyond an extension-suffix check) is passed to os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, filename); '../../etc/cron.d/x.txt' or an absolute path escapes UPLOAD_DIR and writes attacker content anywhere the process can write, leading to RCE (CWE-22/CWE-73, OWASP A01/A03).

Path traversal via upload_id: chunk path os.path.join(CHUNK_DIR, f"{upload_id}.{index}") uses fully unvalidated upload_id, so upload_id='../../var/www/html/shell' writes a controlled file outside CHUNK_DIR (also affects lines 66 and 76) — arbitrary file write (CWE-22).

Extension allowlist is the only content control; no MIME sniffing or magic-byte/content validation, so a polyglot or a .png/.txt containing executable/HTML payload passes — stored XSS or malicious-file serving, and RCE when combined with the path-traversal findings (CWE-434 Unrestricted Upload).

Declared size limit is never enforced: MAX_FILE_MB / file_size_ok() exist but receive_chunk and assemble impose no per-chunk size, no chunk-count cap, and no cumulative-bytes accounting, allowing unbounded disk writes -> disk-exhaustion DoS (CWE-770/CWE-400, OWASP A04).

verify_checksum does content = f.read(), loading the entire file (up to 200MB, unbounded given the unenforced size cap) into memory; concurrent calls cause memory-exhaustion DoS — digest should be computed via streaming chunked reads (CWE-400).

verify_checksum is a public entry point that open()s a caller-supplied stored_path with no validation; if reachable from user input it is an arbitrary file-read / path-traversal oracle and should resolve an ID to a known-safe path instead of accepting raw paths (same applies to file_size_ok at line 90) (CWE-22).

Client-supplied checksum provides no integrity guarantee against a malicious uploader (attacker submits the hash of their own payload); combined with assemble storing+returning the file (lines 63-72) BEFORE any checksum/size gate, the malicious file is already live on disk regardless of later verification (TOCTOU / weak integrity, OWASP A08).

start() unconditionally overwrites self._sessions[upload_id] and chunk paths are keyed solely on the client-chosen upload_id with no auth/ownership binding, so a guessed or reused upload_id lets one client clobber or hijack another's session and interleave chunks (session fixation / cross-tenant interference, CWE-384/CWE-639); the module has no authentication or per-user namespacing.

open(dest_path, 'wb') (and the chunk writes at line 49) follow symlinks; an attacker who can pre-create a symlink at a predictable upload/chunk path (enabled by the predictable {upload_id} naming and path-traversal vectors) causes the write to follow it, overwriting arbitrary files — open with O_NOFOLLOW/O_EXCL (CWE-59 symlink-following).

start() validates only total_chunks < 1 with no upper bound; a very large total_chunks (e.g. 2^31) is accepted, enabling many small-chunk allocations and inflating loop/range work in is_complete/assemble/_cleanup — resource-control gap (CWE-770).

Digest equality uses a plain != comparison rather than hmac.compare_digest; a non-constant-time compare on a security-relevant value is a minor timing-side-channel hygiene issue (CWE-208) — low impact here since the digest is not a secret.

assemble trusts the in-memory received set but reads chunk files from disk with no existence/size re-validation and no error handling; an externally removed or truncated chunk yields an unhandled exception or a silently corrupt assembled file (integrity/robustness, CWE-754).

Off-by-one (non-security, deferred to logic critic): assemble loops range(total_chunks - 1) = indices 0..total_chunks-2, so the final chunk (total_chunks-1) is never concatenated, producing a truncated output file even though is_complete required all chunks.
